title: Dundee cake
subtitle: Scottish light fruit cake topped with concentric rings of almonds
excerpt: A Scottish light fruit cake made with butter, eggs, dried fruit, and citrus peel, distinguished by the pattern of whole blanched almonds arranged in concentric circles on top. Lighter and less rich than Christmas cake, with a moist, close crumb and a long shelf life.
sourceNotes: Dundee cake is a Scottish baking tradition documented from at least the 19th century. Florence White, Good Things in England (1932; Project Gutenberg), includes a recipe for Dundee cake as a distinct Scottish regional bake. The defining characteristics are the lighter texture compared to a Christmas cake (less fruit, no icing), the use of mixed citrus peel and glace cherries, and the unmistakable topping of whole blanched almonds arranged in rings. The recipe here follows the classic Scottish proportions: one-to-one butter and sugar, a generous dried fruit mix, and Scotch whisky optional.

recipe.freezeNotes: Wrap cooled cake in greaseproof paper and foil; freeze for up to 3 months. Defrost at room temperature.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Like all fruit cakes, Dundee cake improves with age. Bake 1 to 2 weeks ahead and store wrapped in a cool place.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: conventional oven. Fan at 140°C. The long, low bake is necessary to cook through the dense fruit cake without burning the almond topping. Cover loosely with foil if the top browns too quickly.

recipeTools.notes: 20 cm deep round tin, greased and double-lined with baking paper

glossaryTerms.definition: The characteristic topping of whole blanched almonds arranged in concentric rings on the surface of a Dundee cake before baking. The almonds are pressed lightly into the raw batter. During baking they colour to a pale gold while the cake rises around them. The pattern is functional as well as decorative: the almonds create a firm crust that protects the top of the cake through the long bake.

content.text: Dundee cake is lighter than a Christmas cake but keeps just as well. The

is the visual signature: whole blanched almonds arranged in rings before the cake goes in the oven. Rinse the glacé cherries and pat dry before adding to the batter; the syrup coating makes them sink. The cake needs a double layer of paper lining and a long, low bake.

attrs.prepNote: optional, or replace with orange juice

content.text: Heat the oven to 160°C conventional (140°C fan). Grease a deep 20 cm round tin and line with a double layer of baking paper on the sides and base. Beat the softened butter and caster sugar until pale and fluffy. Add the eggs one at a time, beating well after each; a spoonful of flour with the later eggs helps prevent curdling. Sift in the flour, ground almonds, baking powder, and mixed spice; fold together. Stir in the citrus zests, dried fruit, and brandy if using.

Spoon the batter into the prepared tin and level the surface. Arrange the blanched almonds in concentric rings across the top of the cake, pressing them lightly into the batter. Bake for 80 to 100 minutes until a skewer comes out clean from the centre. If the top colours too deeply before the 60-minute mark, lay a piece of foil loosely over the top. Leave to cool completely in the tin before turning out.
